text: Meta-analysis of 23 randomized trials on exercise and depressive symptoms in adults with mild to moderate depression. Total sample size was 3,140 participants. Random-effects models were used. The pooled effect favored exercise for reducing depressive symptoms, with moderate heterogeneity. Publication bias was assessed with funnel plots.

draft.bottomLine: This meta-analysis supports the idea that exercise can reduce depressive symptoms in adults with mild to moderate depression. Overall, the evidence feels meaningfully stronger here than in a single trial, although the size and consistency of benefit still vary across studies.

whatPaperActuallyShows.observedResult: The pooled effect favored exercise for reducing depressive symptoms

keyFindings.finding: Across trials, exercise was associated with lower depressive symptom scores.
keyFindings.effectDirection: Symptoms improved on average with exercise
keyFindings.plainEnglishMeaning: Exercise appears to be a credible part of the evidence base for improving depressive symptoms.

trustRating.reason: This is a meta-analysis of randomized trials with a large combined sample, which gives the question a stronger evidence base than a single study.
trustRating.citationUse: Reasonable to cite as strong support for exercise as part of depression care, with normal caveats about variation across interventions and populations.

limitationsAndGotchas.limitation: The included exercise programs varied across trials.
limitationsAndGotchas.whyItMatters: Not all exercise interventions are equally effective, so the pooled average may hide important differences.
limitationsAndGotchas.practicalConsequence: The result supports exercise broadly, but not a single exact protocol for every person.
limitationsAndGotchas.whatWouldStrengthenIt: More head-to-head trials and clearer subgroup analyses would sharpen the practical guidance.

realWorldMeaning.summary: This is useful evidence for exercise as a real part of the depression-treatment conversation, although it still belongs inside a broader care context.
realWorldMeaning.sensibleInterpretation: The sensible takeaway is that exercise has credible support as an adjunct or part of care, not that it replaces all other treatment.

practicalUse.reasoning: The evidence is strong enough to take seriously, but treatment choices still need to fit the individual and the broader clinical picture.
practicalUse.caution: Do not change medication or formal mental-health treatment based on one paper alone.
